regulator: This pillar's centre of gravity is genuinely federal: Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025 defines 'the Supervisory Authority' as 'the federal and local authorities entrusted under the legislation with the supervision of the financial institutions, designated non-financial businesses and professions, virtual asset service providers, and non-profit organizations' -- so each of the five regulators below is, within its own slice, itself 'the Supervisory Authority' for federal AML/CFT/CPF purposes, layering its own AML rulebook and enforcement on top of the shared federal statute. VARA (Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority -- Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC) / the CMA (Capital Market Authority -- federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026) / the DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority -- DIFC only) / the FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority -- ADGM only) / the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE -- hosts the one CBUAE Rulebook document, the UAE Virtual Assets Travel Rule, that expressly applies to VASPs 'within the territorial scope of the UAE Federal and Emirate Laws, any Free Zone and any Financial Free Zone', i.e. across all five slices at once). Two further federal bodies matter for this pillar specifically and are not VASP licensors: the UAE Financial Intelligence Unit (the 'Unit', operator of the goAML reporting platform) and the Executive Office for Control and Non-Proliferation (EOCN, defined in Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025 itself as 'concerned with the implementation of targeted financial sanctions within the State').
instruments: Federal Decree by Law No. (10) of 2025 Regarding Anti-Money Laundering, and Combating the Financing of Terrorism and Proliferation Financing -- Status: In-Force, Effective from 14/10/2025, per direct fetch of the live CBUAE Rulebook page. Repeals and replaces Federal Decree-Law No. (20) of 2018 (as amended by Federal Decree-Law No. (26) of 2021 and Federal Decree-Law No. (7) of 2024); its own recitals cite, among other background statutes, Federal Law No. (7) of 2014 Regarding Combating Terrorist Crimes. Chapter One (Article 1) defines 'the Supervisory Authority' broadly as federal-or-local, defines 'the Unit' as the Financial Intelligence Unit established by the Decree-Law, and defines 'the Executive Office' as the Executive Office for Control and Non-Proliferation (EOCN), 'concerned with the implementation of targeted financial sanctions within the State'; Chapter Seven (confirmed by direct fetch, Article on Supervisory Authorities and Administrative Penalties) gives each Supervisory Authority, 'within their respective areas of competence', power to impose administrative penalties -- warnings, activity suspension/restriction, director suspension, incremental fines for repeat violations, and publication of penalties -- on the Financial Institutions, DNFBPs, Virtual Asset Service Providers, and NPOs under its own supervision; confirmed by direct fetch that no VASP may operate 'without obtaining a license, registration, or enrolment from the Competent Authority or from the relevant Supervisory Authority, as the case may be'.

Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025 Regarding the Executive Regulations of Federal Decree by Law No. (10) of 2025 -- Status: In-Force, Effective from 14/12/2025 (two months after the parent Decree-Law's own 14/10/2025 commencement), per direct fetch of the live CBUAE Rulebook page. Supersedes Cabinet Resolution No. (10) of 2019 (as amended by Cabinet Resolution No. (24) of 2022), the executive regulation of the repealed 2018 law. Confirmed by direct fetch: Article 1 defines 'Virtual Asset Service Providers' as 'Any person who, as a commercial activity, conducts one or more Virtual Asset activities or related operations for the benefit of, or on behalf of, another natural or legal person'; Chapter Two lists five Virtual Asset Activities that trigger the definition -- exchange between Virtual Assets and fiat currencies, exchange between Virtual Assets, transfer of Virtual Assets, safekeeping/administration of Virtual Assets or the instruments controlling them, and participation in/provision of financial services related to an issuer's offer or sale of a Virtual Asset -- the FATF-standard five-activity list.

The United Arab Emirates' Virtual Assets Travel Rule -- a standalone document on the CBUAE Rulebook, Status: In-Force, confirmed by direct fetch. Its own 'Application' clause states in terms: 'The following provisions shall apply with respect to Virtual Asset Service Providers within the territorial scope of the UAE Federal and Emirate Laws, any Free Zone and any Financial Free Zone' -- i.e. it binds VASPs regardless of which of the five regulators licenses them, including inside the DIFC and ADGM. Article (1) requires an Originator's VASP to collect, verify and transmit originator/beneficiary name, wallet/account number (or a traceable reference number), and the originator's address/national ID or travel-document number/customer ID/date and place of birth, for qualifying Virtual Asset Transfers, with simplified treatment below a threshold and full verification duties above it (the threshold amount, and record-keeping duties, are defined by cross-reference to 'the Decision' -- see the open item on this document's unreconciled internal cross-reference below); Article (4) requires suspicious activity/transaction reports on Virtual Asset Transfers to be filed with the UAE Financial Intelligence Unit; Article (5) provides that 'No UAE Virtual Asset Service Provider shall Execute a Virtual Asset Transfer of a Privacy Token due to their potential use in obscuring transaction details, thereby increasing the risk of money laundering, terrorist financing and proliferation financing' -- a nationwide, cross-free-zone prohibition; Article (6) lets 'the Supervisory Authorities' (plural) issue joint interpretive guidance.

VARA Compliance and Risk Management Rulebook, Part III -- Anti-Money Laundering and Combating the Financing of Terrorism -- current version V.1, effective 19 June 2025 (per the rulebook's own version history, latest update dated 19 May 2025) -- applicable under VARA's rules, in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC). Confirmed by direct fetch: Part III covers appointment/duties of a Money Laundering Reporting Officer, AML/CFT policies and procedures, AML/CFT controls (Section C: VASPs must maintain 'effective AML/CFT controls and systems ... which can adequately manage the AML/CFT risks', including use of 'distributed ledger analytics tools and other investigative tools', reviewed and documented on an ongoing basis, and must design transaction-monitoring thresholds with reference to the FATF's 'Virtual Assets Red Flags Indicators of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (September 2020)'), risk assessments, client due diligence, suspicious transaction monitoring and reporting, FATF Travel Rule compliance (Section G), targeted financial sanctions compliance, and record keeping.

VARA Rulebook, 'Federal AML/CFT Laws' page -- confirmed by direct fetch -- VARA's own list of the federal instruments its licensees must additionally comply with: the UAE Virtual Assets Travel Rule; Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025 (described by VARA as superseding Cabinet Decision No. (10) of 2019); Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025 (described by VARA as replacing Federal Decree-Law No. (20) of 2018); Cabinet Decision No. (74) of 2020 Concerning the Regulation of Terrorism Lists and Implementation of UN Security Council Resolutions; and Federal Decree-Law No. (7) of 2014 On Combating Terrorism Offences -- direct textual confirmation, from VARA's own rulebook, that VARA treats this federal layer as binding on top of its own Part III rules.

VARA Rulebook, Part III.G -- FATF Travel Rule -- confirmed by direct fetch: sets an AED 3,500 aggregate-value threshold for full Travel Rule data-verification duties (below which simplified information accompanies the transfer, absent suspicion), requires collection of Originator name, account/wallet number and address, and Beneficiary name and account/wallet number, and states VASPs 'must comply with all Federal AML-CFT Laws, including but not limited to Travel Rule requirements as prescribed in Federal AML-CFT Laws ... as may be incrementally defined by VARA from time to time' -- VARA's own rule text expressly deferring to the federal layer rather than restating it.

DFSA Rulebook, Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing and Sanctions Module (AML), current consolidated version VER30/04-26 -- applicable under DFSA rules, only within the DIFC. Confirmed by direct fetch of the module's chapter listing: AML 1-16 cover Introduction, Overview and Purpose, Interpretation and Terminology, Applying a Risk-Based Approach, Business Risk Assessment, Customer Risk Assessment, Customer Due Diligence, Reliance and Outsourcing, Correspondent Banking/Electronic Fund Transfers/Audit (AML 9), Sanctions and Other International Obligations, Money Laundering Reporting Officer, AML Training and Awareness, Suspicious Activity Reports, General, DNFBP Registration and Supervision, and Transitional Rules. Within AML 9, confirmed by direct fetch of the chapter's own table of contents, sub-sections AML 9.3A ('Additional requirements for Crypto Token transfers') and AML 9.3B ('Additional requirements for NFT and Utility Token transfers') extend the module's wire-transfer/travel-rule provisions specifically to Crypto Token and NFT/Utility Token transfers -- this research confirmed the sub-section headings and their position in the module exist as fetched but could not retrieve the numbered sub-rules' operative text this session (see open_items).

FSRA Anti-Money Laundering and Sanctions Rules and Guidance (AML Rulebook), version VER11.210526, genuine PDF hosted on assets.adgm.com -- applicable under FSRA rules, only within ADGM. Confirmed by direct fetch and extraction of the live PDF: Rule 1.1.1 states 'The AML Rulebook is made in recognition of the application of the Federal AML Legislation in ADGM' and 'Nothing in the AML Rulebook affects the operation of Federal AML Legislation' -- FSRA's own rule text confirming that the federal AML/CFT statute (now Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025) reaches into ADGM notwithstanding FSRA's separate rulebook; Rule 1.2.1 applies the AML Rulebook to every 'Relevant Person' (Authorised Persons, Recognised Bodies, Representative Offices, DNFBPs, NPOs) for activities carried out in or from ADGM, with a chapter-applicability table by Relevant Person type. Chapter 10, 'Correspondent Banking, Electronic Fund Transfers, Virtual Assets and Fiat-Referenced Tokens Transfers and the Travel Rule, Audit and Anonymous Accounts', Section 10.3 'Transfers of Virtual Assets and Fiat-Referenced Tokens and the travel rule' -- Rule 10.3.1 applies the section to all Authorised Persons and Recognised Bodies, subject to stated exclusions (message/support-system-only providers; Financial-Institution-to-Financial-Institution transfers on own account, subject to Rule 10.3.4).

FSRA Final Notice against Aarna Capital Limited, dated 13 December 2024, genuine PDF hosted on assets.adgm.com -- confirmed by direct fetch and full-text extraction: a US$504,000 financial penalty (reduced 20% from US$630,000 for early settlement) under section 232 of the Financial Services and Markets Regulations 2015, for failing, over the period 8 June 2017 to 13 January 2023, to maintain adequate AML systems and controls under the FSRA's Anti-Money Laundering and Sanctions Rules and Guidance -- specifically inadequate risk-based customer assessment, inadequate documentation of that assessment, inadequate CDD/EDD record-keeping, and inadequate review frequency. This research's own full-text search of the Notice found zero occurrences of 'virtual asset' or 'crypto' -- Aarna Capital Limited is a multi-asset brokerage, and this action illustrates the FSRA's general AML enforcement mechanism as it exists inside ADGM, not a virtual-asset-specific action (see open_items for the corresponding check on a DFSA action wrongly characterized by secondary media as AML-related).

CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 -- reported by multiple independent secondary legal-commentary sources (Clyde & Co, Cleary Gottlieb, King & Spalding, Mielo Group and others; not independently verified against CMA primary text in this research pass, the same limitation already logged in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar) to introduce, alongside its eight-activity virtual-asset licensing framework, five core modules including a dedicated AML/CFT module, and to position 'AML/CFT compliance ... as a foundational licensing requirement, rather than a supplementary obligation' for virtual-asset firms; applicable, if and as reported, under the CMA's federal framework, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM. This research also could not, in repeated attempts, reach CMA primary text (uaecma.gov.ae) setting out the AML/CFT module's specific rules -- see open_items.

VARA circular to all Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) in the Emirate of Dubai, dated 24 November 2025, titled 'Publication of UAE Federal Decree-Law on AML/CFT/CPF (2025) -- Mandatory GAP Assessment for VASPs' -- confirmed by direct fetch of the source PDF -- notifies VASPs of the then-newly-effective Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025 and layers VARA-specific, dated compliance milestones on top of the federal statute: a completed, clause-by-clause GAP Assessment Report and board-approved Remediation Plan due within 60 calendar days of the circular; implementation of priority remedial fixes and staff/role-based/senior-management training within 90 days; and closure of remaining gaps with a signed-off completion plan within 120 days, all backed by a recordkeeping obligation (internal working papers, testing results, Board minutes) and a stated warning that non-compliance 'may result in enforcement actions under the Regulations and Federal AML-CFT Laws.'
standing_summary: Unlike every other pillar in this jurisdiction, AML/CFT/CPF is the one area with a genuinely federal instrument that this research confirms, by direct fetch of its own definitions, was designed to bind all five regulatory slices at once: Federal Decree by Law No. (10) of 2025 Regarding Anti-Money Laundering, and Combating the Financing of Terrorism and Proliferation Financing, in force from 14 October 2025, repealing the former Federal Decree-Law No. (20) of 2018 (as twice amended, in 2021 and 2024). This research fetched the live consolidated text of the new Decree-Law directly from the CBUAE Rulebook and confirmed that its own Article (1) defines 'the Supervisory Authority' as 'the federal and local authorities entrusted under the legislation with the supervision of the financial institutions, designated non-financial businesses and professions, virtual asset service providers, and non-profit organizations' -- language broad enough, and evidently intended, to cover VARA (a local Dubai authority), the CMA (federal), and the DFSA and FSRA (the two Financial Free Zone regulators) each as 'the Supervisory Authority' for the licensees within its own slice, with the Decree-Law's Chapter Seven directly confirmed to give each Supervisory Authority power, 'within their respective areas of competence', to impose administrative penalties on the Financial Institutions, DNFBPs, Virtual Asset Service Providers and NPOs it supervises. The Decree-Law's own recitals also define 'the Unit' (the UAE Financial Intelligence Unit, which operates the goAML reporting platform) and 'the Executive Office' (the Executive Office for Control and Non-Proliferation, EOCN, 'concerned with the implementation of targeted financial sanctions within the State') directly in the federal statute itself, rather than leaving them to a Supervisory Authority's own rulebook -- confirming both bodies as federal, cross-cutting institutions rather than any one regulator's creature. Its implementing regulation, Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025, in force from 14 December 2025 and confirmed by this research's own direct fetch, supersedes the former Cabinet Resolution No. (10) of 2019 and defines 'Virtual Asset Service Providers' and the five FATF-standard Virtual Asset Activities (fiat/VA and VA/VA exchange, transfer, safekeeping/administration, and issuer-facing financial services) that trigger VASP status for AML/CFT purposes federally, independent of which of the five regulators separately licenses the activity. Separately from that Decree-Law/Resolution pair, this research directly fetched a further standalone CBUAE Rulebook document -- 'The United Arab Emirates' Virtual Assets Travel Rule' -- whose own Application clause states verbatim that its provisions 'shall apply with respect to Virtual Asset Service Providers within the territorial scope of the UAE Federal and Emirate Laws, any Free Zone and any Financial Free Zone', meaning this one document, hosted on the CBUAE's own domain, is the clearest example in this pillar of a rule that genuinely reaches across all five regulatory slices including the DIFC and ADGM: it requires Originator/Beneficiary identification data to accompany Virtual Asset Transfers (Article 1), requires suspicious-activity/transaction reporting to the Financial Intelligence Unit (Article 4), and flatly bans, UAE-wide including inside every free zone, the execution of a Virtual Asset Transfer of a Privacy Token (Article 5) -- language this research also found echoed, independently, in VARA's own rulebook. On top of this federal layer, each regulator runs its own AML/CFT rulebook and its own enforcement inside its own slice. Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC), the Compliance and Risk Management Rulebook's Part III sets VASP-facing AML/CFT obligations (Money Laundering Reporting Officer appointment, policies/procedures, AML/CFT controls requiring documented use of distributed-ledger analytics tools benchmarked against FATF's 2020 Virtual Assets Red Flags guidance, risk assessments, CDD, STR monitoring/reporting, an AED 3,500 Travel Rule threshold, and targeted-financial-sanctions compliance), while VARA's own 'Federal AML/CFT Laws' rulebook page, fetched directly, explicitly lists the Travel Rule, Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025, Federal Decree-Law No. (10) of 2025, Cabinet Decision No. (74) of 2020 (terrorism lists/UNSC resolutions), and Federal Decree-Law No. (7) of 2014 (Combating Terrorism Offences) as binding on top of Part III -- VARA's own text confirming the layered structure described above. On enforcement, this research fetched two of VARA's own 2025-2026 regulatory notices directly: against Morpheus Software Technology FZE ('Fuze', VARA Ref. VL/23/10/002, licensed 19 October 2023), a notice dated 18 August 2025 following an investigation that commenced 15 April 2025, finding 'failures in AML programme controls, related governance, compliance and internal systems and controls', unlicensed virtual-asset activity, and failure to disclose material facts to VARA, resulting in an undisclosed financial penalty, cease-and-desist measures, and the appointment of a Skilled Person to oversee Fuze's own proposed remediation plan; and against CoinMENA FZE (VARA Ref. VL/23/09/001, a VA Broker-Dealer Services licensee since 30 November 2023), a notice dated 22 June 2026 finding 'administrative issues with internal systems and controls, resulting in compliance failures' in CoinMENA's AML programme, again resulting in an undisclosed financial penalty, with CoinMENA stated to have cooperated and proposed a remediation plan. Under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM, CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 is reported by multiple secondary legal-commentary sources -- not independently verified against CMA primary text this session, the same limitation already logged for this decision in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar -- to include a dedicated AML/CFT module among five core modules of its new eight-activity virtual-asset licensing framework, with AML/CFT positioned as 'a foundational licensing requirement'. Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, this research directly fetched the chapter structure of the Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorist Financing and Sanctions Module (AML), current consolidated version VER30/04-26, confirming that its Chapter 9 (Correspondent Banking, Electronic Fund Transfers and Audit) contains dedicated sub-sections AML 9.3A ('Additional requirements for Crypto Token transfers') and AML 9.3B ('Additional requirements for NFT and Utility Token transfers') -- confirming the DFSA extends its wire-transfer/travel-rule regime to crypto assets by name, though this research could not retrieve the operative numbered sub-rule text of 9.3A/9.3B this session. Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, this research directly fetched and extracted the full text of the FSRA's own Anti-Money Laundering and Sanctions Rules and Guidance (AML Rulebook, VER11.210526): its Rule 1.1.1 states outright that the Rulebook 'is made in recognition of the application of the Federal AML Legislation in ADGM' and that 'Nothing in the AML Rulebook affects the operation of Federal AML Legislation' -- FSRA's own text confirming, from inside the free zone, that the federal Decree-Law reaches ADGM notwithstanding FSRA's separate rulebook -- and its Chapter 10.3, 'Transfers of Virtual Assets and Fiat-Referenced Tokens and the travel rule', applies to all Authorised Persons and Recognised Bodies subject to stated carve-outs. On FSRA enforcement, this research directly fetched and full-text-searched the FSRA's Final Notice against Aarna Capital Limited (13 December 2024, US$504,000 penalty, reduced 20% for early settlement from US$630,000, for AML systems-and-controls failures -- inadequate risk-based customer assessment, documentation, CDD/EDD recordkeeping and review frequency -- over 8 June 2017 to 13 January 2023) and confirmed it contains zero mentions of 'virtual asset' or 'crypto': Aarna Capital is a multi-asset brokerage, and this action illustrates the FSRA's general AML enforcement mechanism inside ADGM rather than a virtual-asset-specific case. This research also directly fetched a DFSA Decision Notice that a secondary source (a law-firm client alert) had characterized as an 'AML-related fine' -- against Al Ramz Capital LLC, 13 June 2024, USD25,000 -- and found, on the primary document's own text, that the finding was in fact a failure to notify the DFSA of suspected Market Abuse (wash trades) under Rule 3.4.5(1) of the DFSA's Recognition Module (REC), with no AML/CFT rule cited anywhere in the Notice; this pillar accordingly does not include that action as an AML/CFT enforcement example, and flags the secondary source's characterization as inaccurate (see open_items). Internationally, the UAE was removed from the FATF's list of 'Jurisdictions under Increased Monitoring' (the 'grey list') on 23 February 2024, having been added 4 March 2022; this research relies on multiple independent secondary sources (Norton Rose Fulbright, Herbert Smith Freehills, Moody's) for this fact because fatf-gafi.org itself returned HTTP 403 to every direct fetch attempt this session (see open_items) -- FATF is not one of this jurisdiction's five registered VASP regulators and this fact is included as international-standard-setter context for the pillar, not as a primary-sourced regulatory rule. Finally, this research notes but does not resolve an internal inconsistency in the CBUAE's own Rulebook: the Virtual Assets Travel Rule document's own Definitions section still defines 'the Decision' -- the instrument that supplies its AED threshold, STR-referral mechanics and risk-assessment cross-references -- as 'Cabinet Decision No. (10) of 2019 ... as amended by Cabinet Resolution No. (24) of 2022', even though this research separately confirmed, from a different CBUAE Rulebook page, that Cabinet Decision No. (10) of 2019's own Part 4 (Virtual Asset Service Providers) is now marked 'Status: Repealed'; this reads as the CBUAE's Travel Rule document's cross-reference not yet having been updated to point to Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025, the successor regulation, rather than any live legal ambiguity, but it is logged here exactly as observed rather than silently corrected (see open_items).

DFSA: Decision Notice -- Al Ramz Capital LLC, USD25,000 penalty (13 Jun 2024) -- a Market Abuse notification failure under REC 3.4.5(1), not an AML/CFT rule; included here only to document why this pillar does not cite it as an AML/CFT enforcement example
VARA circular to VASPs -- Publication of UAE Federal Decree-Law on AML/CFT/CPF (2025) -- Mandatory GAP Assessment for VASPs (24 Nov 2025), PDF

open_items: The CBUAE Rulebook's 'United Arab Emirates' Virtual Assets Travel Rule' document -- confirmed In-Force and confirmed to state the broadest free-zone-inclusive scope of any instrument in this pillar -- itself defines 'the Decision', the instrument supplying its AED threshold and risk-assessment cross-references, as 'Cabinet Decision No. (10) of 2019 ... as amended by Cabinet Resolution No. (24) of 2022'. This research separately confirmed, from the CBUAE Rulebook's own 'Part 4 -- Virtual Asset Service Providers' page (a page nested under that same Cabinet Decision No. (10) of 2019), that this instrument is now marked 'Status: Repealed'. This is logged as an unreconciled internal cross-reference on the CBUAE's own Rulebook -- most plausibly the Travel Rule document's Definitions section not yet having been updated to point to Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025, the actual successor Executive Regulation -- rather than a live legal ambiguity, but it should be re-verified directly against CBUAE's Rulebook before the Travel Rule's threshold/reporting mechanics are treated as authoritatively linked to a specific current legal instrument.

This research attempted, and failed, to retrieve the operative numbered rule text of DFSA AML 9.3A ('Additional requirements for Crypto Token transfers') and AML 9.3B ('Additional requirements for NFT and Utility Token transfers') -- direct fetches of guessed sub-rule URLs on dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com returned the site's generic navigation shell rather than rule text. This card confirms these sub-sections exist, by name and position, within the current DFSA AML Module (VER30/04-26) chapter structure, fetched directly, but does not quote or paraphrase their specific requirements; that should be done in a future pass once a working URL for the sub-rule pages themselves is found (dfsa.ae itself also returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch attempts, as already logged in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar).

CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026's reported AML/CFT module -- including its specific rules, thresholds, and any named licensing conditions -- is sourced in this card only from secondary legal-commentary publications (Clyde & Co, Cleary Gottlieb, King & Spalding, Mielo Group, among others), not from directly fetched CMA primary text, mirroring the identical limitation already logged for this same Decision in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar. Repeated attempts this session to locate readable primary CMA text for the Decision's AML/CFT module specifically (as distinct from its stablecoin-relevant provisions, previously attempted) did not succeed.

This research fetched a DFSA Decision Notice (Al Ramz Capital LLC, 13 June 2024, USD25,000) that a secondary source (globalinvestigations.blog / National Law Review) had characterized, in an article titled 'The FSRA and the DFSA Issue New AML-Related Fines', as an AML-related enforcement action. On direct reading of the primary Decision Notice, the finding was in fact a failure to notify the DFSA of suspected Market Abuse (wash trades) under Rule 3.4.5(1) of the Recognition Module (REC) of the DFSA Rulebook -- no AML/CFT rule is cited anywhere in the Notice. This pillar does not include the Al Ramz case as a DFSA AML/CFT enforcement example, and this research did not find, in the time available, a genuine DFSA enforcement action against a named entity for an AML/CFT-rule (as opposed to REC/Market-Abuse-rule) breach; a future pass should check the DFSA's own enforcement/regulatory-actions listing directly (dfsa.ae returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch attempts this session) for one before this pillar is treated as having a confirmed DFSA-side enforcement example.

The UAE's removal from the FATF grey list (23 February 2024) is stated in this card on the strength of multiple independent secondary sources (Norton Rose Fulbright, Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer, Moody's) rather than a direct fetch of fatf-gafi.org, which returned HTTP 403 (a live Cloudflare bot-block) to every direct fetch attempt this session, including with the project's own descriptive User-Agent and with a generic browser User-Agent. FATF is not one of this jurisdiction's five registered VASP regulators (config/jurisdictions/uae.json's regulators[] list), so this fact is retained as international-standard-setter context for the pillar rather than as a primary-sourced regulatory claim, consistent with the editorial rule that non-primary sources may corroborate but never solely found a claim -- if this pillar is revisited, a working fetch route to fatf-gafi.org (or to MENAFATF's own site, not attempted this session) should replace this open item.

This research relied on two UAE federal government domains not currently listed in config/jurisdictions/uae.json's regulators[] official_domains -- uaefiu.gov.ae (the UAE Financial Intelligence Unit, source for the pre-2018 legislative history: Federal Law No. (4) of 2002 as the first UAE money-laundering statute) and uaeiec.gov.ae (the Executive Office for Control and Non-Proliferation's own domain, source for goAML/CNMR/PNMR reporting-mechanics detail) -- for contextual, non-load-bearing facts only; every load-bearing factual claim in this card's standing_summary is instead sourced to a registered domain (rulebook.centralbank.ae/centralbank.ae, rulebooks.vara.ae/vara.ae, dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com, assets.adgm.com/adgm.com). Both uaefiu.gov.ae and uaeiec.gov.ae are genuine, fetchable UAE federal government sources and are strong future registration candidates for this jurisdiction's config, mirroring the registry-first convention already logged in the Japan jurisdiction's aml_cft_enforcement pillar for JAFIC/npa.go.jp -- but registering them is a config change outside this research pass's and this pillar-state file's own remit.

This pillar covers the federal AML/CFT/CPF statute and each of the five regulators' own AML rulebook/enforcement layer as they apply to Virtual Asset Service Providers and virtual-asset activity specifically. It does not attempt full coverage of the federal AML/CFT regime as it applies to traditional (non-virtual-asset) Financial Institutions and DNFBPs generally -- e.g. the CBUAE's extensive library of sector-specific AML/CFT guidance for exchange houses, hawala providers, real estate, insurance, and correspondent banking, confirmed to exist on the CBUAE Rulebook's AML/CFT landing page but not analyzed in this pass -- since that lies outside this jurisdiction's virtual-asset/digital-asset relevance-keyword scope per config/jurisdictions/uae.json.
